title: xTool Creative Space (XCS) vs xTool Studio
summary: Overview of xTool's two control applications (XCS and xTool Studio) and the shared eight-step design-to-cut workflow.
content:
# xTool Creative Space (XCS) vs xTool Studio

## 1. XCS vs. xTool Studio

xTool has two distinct desktop/mobile applications. They are **separate installable applications, not a rebrand of one another** — xTool Studio is the successor product, and XCS has been frozen at its final version.

### xTool Creative Space (XCS)

- **What it is**: xTool's original laser/craft-cutting control software, used to import designs, set cut/engrave/score parameters, frame jobs, and send them to the machine.
- **Status**: Frozen at its final version, **XCS Desktop V2.7 (specifically V2.7.22, released around mid-to-late June 2025)**. No further feature development is planned for XCS — new features and machine support are going into xTool Studio instead.
- **Which machines use it**: All legacy and current-generation xTool machines were originally designed around XCS, including D1 Pro, S1, P2, M1/M1 Ultra, and F1/F1 Ultra. Older machines may continue to rely on XCS indefinitely since Studio compatibility rolled out gradually by machine.
- **Native file format**: `.xcs` — xTool's proprietary project file format, storing design layers, processing-type assignments (cut/score/engrave), and per-layer parameters.
- **Platforms**: Windows and macOS desktop; iOS and Android mobile companion apps also exist for basic job sending/monitoring.
- **Import formats**: Common vector formats (SVG, DXF), common raster/bitmap formats (JPG, PNG, BMP), and PDF.
- **Export formats**: `.xcs` project files; processed jobs are sent directly to the machine rather than exported to a universal machine format (no G-code export in the consumer UI).

### xTool Studio

- **What it is**: xTool's next-generation design and machine-control software, intended to eventually replace XCS across the product line. Positioned as a more modern, actively developed application with expanded design tools and a cleaner processing-parameter UI.
- **Status**: Launched as **Studio Desktop V1.0 on September 4, 2025**; as of this writing it has progressed well past **V1.7.30**, which introduced a new native project format.
- **Which machines use it**: Newer/actively-supported machines are prioritized for Studio support first; official guidance is to check the specific machine's support page to confirm Studio compatibility, since rollout has been staged rather than simultaneous across the whole product line. D1 Pro, S1, P2, M1/M1 Ultra, and F1/F1 Ultra are all Studio-compatible as of current versions.
- **Native file formats**:
  - `.xcs` — Studio can still open/save the legacy XCS format for cross-compatibility with older projects and with users still on XCS.
  - `.xs` — a **new native format introduced in Studio V1.7.30**. Important: **`.xs` files are NOT backward-compatible** with XCS or with Studio versions prior to 1.7 — a project saved as `.xs` in a current Studio version cannot be opened in XCS or in an older Studio release.
- **Platforms**: Windows and macOS desktop; mobile app support following a similar staged rollout to machine support.
- **Import formats**: Same baseline as XCS (SVG, DXF, JPG, PNG, BMP, PDF), with Studio adding broader/refined vector-format handling as part of its expanded design toolset.
- **Export formats**: `.xcs` and `.xs` project files; direct-to-machine job sending as with XCS.

### Key Workflow Differences

- Studio reorganizes the processing-parameter panel and adds a **Polygon framing mode** in addition to the Rect and Outline framing modes both applications share (see Framing below).
- Studio's design toolset is more expansive (more native vector editing tools) versus XCS's comparatively minimal design surface, which historically leaned on imported designs from external vector editors.
- Users migrating projects from XCS to Studio should save/export as `.xcs` if they need the file to remain readable by anyone still on XCS or on Studio versions before 1.7; only use `.xs` once fully committed to the newer format.

> Note: exact current version numbers for XCS and Studio should be reconfirmed against support.xtool.com at time of reading, since both are (or were, in XCS's case) actively versioned — the numbers above reflect the most recently confirmed releases at time of research.

## 2. Core Processing Workflow

Both XCS and xTool Studio follow the same fundamental job pipeline, though the UI details differ:

1. **Import or design** — Bring in a design via file import (SVG, DXF, JPG, PNG, BMP, PDF) or create one natively with the software's built-in design tools (text, shapes, vector drawing).
2. **Arrange on the virtual canvas** — Position, scale, and rotate design elements to match the intended physical placement on the material inside the machine's work area.
3. **Assign a processing type per layer/object** — Each vector or bitmap object is assigned one of the core processing types:
   - **Cut**: vector-only; cuts completely through the material along the path.
   - **Score**: vector-only; a lighter, single-pass outline scribe/etch that does *not* cut through — used for crease lines, half-cuts, or decorative outline marks.
   - **Engrave**: comes in two sub-modes —
     - *Vector/fill engrave*: traces and fills vector shapes at engrave-level power.
     - *Bitmap/raster (grayscale) engrave*: rasterizes a bitmap image line-by-line, varying power or dot duration by pixel brightness to reproduce photos/grayscale artwork.
4. **Set processing parameters** — For the assigned processing type, configure:
   - **Power (%)** — laser output as a percentage of the module's rated wattage.
   - **Speed (mm/s)** — carriage travel speed during processing.
   - **Passes** — number of times the toolpath repeats (default 1; up to 10 for engrave operations, commonly 2-4 for cuts through thicker material).
   - **Bitmap-only parameters** (for raster/grayscale engrave):
     - **DPI** (dots per inch; effective range roughly 1–1270 in-app; 100 lines/cm ≈ 254 DPI).
     - **Dot Duration** (µs) — used primarily on fiber/IR modules (e.g., F1's IR laser); recommended above 100µs, typically 100–500µs.
     - **Bitmap mode** — one of several dithering/halftone algorithms (7 modes available, including Jarvis); Jarvis is recommended for infrared/fiber engraving, while plain grayscale is recommended for blue-diode engraving.
     - **Scan direction** — bi-directional (default, faster) or uni-directional (slower, can reduce banding artifacts on some materials).
5. **Framing** — Before firing the laser for real, run a **Frame** pass: the machine traces the job's bounding boundary at low power (or with a positioning light/camera on camera-equipped machines) so the user can confirm placement on the physical material. Framing modes:
   - **Rect** — traces the rectangular bounding box of the whole job.
   - **Outline** — traces the actual outline of the design content instead of just a rectangle.
   - **Polygon** (Studio only) — traces an arbitrary polygon boundary, useful for irregular material shapes/scraps.
6. **Focus the laser** — Set physical focus (manual focus tool, motorized auto-focus on supported machines, or camera-assisted focus) appropriate to material thickness before processing; incorrect focus is one of the most common causes of poor cut/engrave quality.
7. **Process the job** — Start the actual cut/score/engrave run. The software streams processing instructions to the machine over USB or Wi-Fi as the job executes.
8. **Monitor and retrieve** — Supervise the run (never leave a running laser unattended), then remove the finished material once the job completes and the laser has fully stopped.

title: Laser Safety Essentials
summary: Core xTool laser safety rules covering prohibited materials, ventilation, fire prevention, eye protection, air assist, and a pre-job checklist.
content:
# xTool Laser Safety Essentials

Sources: support.xtool.com/learning-center, xtool.com. Compiled as part of an open-source xTool knowledge base. Every claim below is sourced to a specific official article with a verbatim quote where possible. **This document is a research aggregation, not a substitute for your machine's printed manual or xTool's official safety guide — always read the manual for your specific model before operating it.**

---

## 1. Forbidden and Dangerous Materials (read this section first)

### PVC / Vinyl / Chlorine-Containing Materials — NEVER cut or engrave these

This is the single most important safety rule across all xTool machines. PVC (polyvinyl chloride), vinyl, pleather/artificial leather, and any other chlorine/chloride-containing material release **toxic chlorine gas** when lased, and the resulting hydrochloric acid/corrosive vapor **damages the machine's optics and motion system**.

Verbatim, from the official **Safety Guide for xTool D1 Laser Cutting & Engraving** (support.xtool.com/article/354):

> "Do not engrave or cut any material that contains PVC or vinyl (plastic materials are not recommended). These materials (and other materials containing chlorine/chloride) may generate corrosive vapor that is extremely harmful to human bodies and may cause damage to the machine. Any damage caused by engraving or cutting any material containing PVC or vinyl is not covered by Makeblock's warranty."

The same article's material-hazard table states:

> "PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride) /vinyl/pleather/artificial leather — Emits toxic pure chlorine gas with strong and pungent odors! Never cut this material as it causes the machines to corrode, damages optical devices and the motion control system."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

The **xTool M2 Material Cheat Sheet** (support.xtool.com/article/3538) reiterates this for a current-generation machine and flags a common consumer mix-up:

> "PVC-containing material must never be lasered... ⛔ Don't laser these: These can release toxic fumes or damage the machine — do not cut or engrave them: PVC / chlorine-containing vinyl materials... One common mix-up to flag: printable adhesive vinyl is not the same as PVC you can laser-cut. When buying 'vinyl' materials, look for a PVC-free label — and if you're not sure what it's made of, keep it out of the laser."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/3538

The **xTool F2 FAQ** (support.xtool.com/article/2818) confirms this applies to current-generation machines too:

> "Avoid processing the following materials with xTool F2: PVC and other chlorinated polymers (as they release toxic chlorine gas)..."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/2818

**This is exactly why xTool's hybrid craft machines (M1, M1 Ultra, M2) include a physical cutting blade module** — so that vinyl/HTV/PVC-based craft materials can be cut mechanically instead of with the laser. Always use Blade Cut mode for vinyl-type materials on machines that have it; never attempt to laser-cut vinyl even if the software allows you to select it.

### Official "Not Suitable for Processing" Material List

The official **xTool P2 FAQ** (support.xtool.com/article/825) gives xTool's canonical prohibited-materials list:

> "What materials can't I process with xTool P2? Do not use xTool P2 to process leather containing chromium, polyvinyl chloride, or polyvinyl butyral; and polytetrafluoroethylene, beryllium oxide, and any materials containing halogens, epoxy, or phenolic resins."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/825

The equivalent legacy list (support.xtool.com/article/253 and its zendesk mirror, covering the Laserbox product line but describing the same underlying laser-material chemistry hazards) adds **carbon fiber** explicitly and explains the mechanism:

> "Chromium (VI) contains leather and artificial leather, such as leather goods, and leather products. Carbon fibers (carbon), e.g., carbon fiber products: caps, plates. Polyvinyl chloride (PVC), e.g., plastic products. Polyvinyl butyral (PVB), e.g., laminated glass, film. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE / Teflon), e.g., plastic products. Beryllium oxide, e.g., high conductivity oxide ceramics, beryllium alloys, etc. And materials containing halogens (fluorine, chlorine, bromine, iodine, and astatine), epoxy resins, or phenolic resins. If laser engraving or laser cutting processes are performed on the above materials, dust or gases may be generated that may be hazardous to the laser equipment operator or the laser machine function."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/253

Summary list of officially prohibited/not-recommended materials:

| Material | Why it's dangerous |
|---|---|
| PVC / vinyl / pleather / artificial leather | Releases toxic chlorine gas; corrodes machine and damages optics |
| Chromium-containing leather (chromium-tanned) | Hazardous dust/gas on laser processing |
| Polyvinyl butyral (PVB) — e.g. laminated glass, film | Hazardous dust/gas on laser processing |
| Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE / Teflon) | Hazardous dust/gas on laser processing |
| Beryllium oxide (ceramics, beryllium alloys) | Hazardous dust/gas on laser processing |
| Any halogen-containing material (fluorine, chlorine, bromine, iodine, astatine) | Hazardous dust/gas on laser processing |
| Epoxy resins | Hazardous dust/gas on laser processing |
| Phenolic resins | Hazardous dust/gas on laser processing |
| Carbon fiber / coated carbon fiber | Emits noxious/harmful fumes |

### Other Dangerous Materials (official warnings)

The D1 Safety Guide's material-hazard table (support.xtool.com/article/354) gives verbatim warnings for several additional common craft materials:

> "ABS — Exudes cyanide gas and tends to melt. ABS doesn't work well with a laser cutter as it is apt to melt rather than vaporize, and is likely to catch fire and leaves melted gooey deposits. Besides, ABS is not a good choice for engraving because it tends to melt."
>
> "HDPE — Melts and easily catches fire."
>
> "Polystyrene Foam — Melts and easily catches fire."
>
> "Polypropylene Foam — Melts and easily catches fire."
>
> "Polycarbonate/latex paint — Changes color and catches fire."
>
> "Coated Carbon Fiber — Emits noxious fumes."
>
> "Acrylic — Emits noxious fumes. For your information, most 450nm laser can pass through acrylic. Laser beams emitted by semiconductor laser devices such as xTool D1 and M1 range from 450nm to 460nm in wavelength, which means most laser beams pass through acrylic instead of being absorbed."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

The **F2 FAQ** (support.xtool.com/article/2818) adds fiberglass and glass/reflective-material cautions specific to current hardware:

> "Avoid processing the following materials with xTool F2: ...Carbon fiber and fiberglass (as they emit harmful fumes); Polyethylene film or foam (as they melt easily and deliver poor quality); Bare glass or crystal (as they may easily crack; use black tape if necessary); Highly reflective or fluffy materials, such as cotton (as they may cause focus errors)."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/2818

The **M2 Material Cheat Sheet** (support.xtool.com/article/3538) separately flags **polycarbonate (PC)** and unknown-composition leather/foam:

> "⛔ Don't laser these: ...Polycarbonate (PC); Leather or foam of unknown composition."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/3538

**General rule for any unknown material**: per the D1 Safety Guide:

> "Do not engrave or cut any unknown materials. Vaporization/melting of many materials, which include but are not limited to PVC and polycarbonate, may release harmful smoke."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

If you don't know exactly what a material is made of (no manufacturer spec sheet or MSDS), treat it as unsafe until proven otherwise — the M2 sheet echoes this for foam specifically: "Must confirm the material is safe; PVC/EVA and similar need caution — check the MSDS first."

### Reflective Materials — equipment damage risk

> "Warning: Do not process reflective materials such as mirrors. It is extremely dangerous to get laser beams reflected, which also causes damage to laser devices."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

This extends to highly reflective metals like polished/mirror-finish stainless steel: reflected beam energy can damage a diode laser module (support.xtool.com/article/1716, F1-series material guidance). Always test a small area first and never operate the machine's lid/cover open when processing reflective stock.

---

## 2. Ventilation and Fume Extraction

Laser cutting/engraving vaporizes material and produces smoke, fumes, and fine particulates regardless of how "safe" the material is — proper ventilation is required for every job, not just risky materials.

> "Lasers create fumes and smoke. It is important to create a proper ventilation system to ensure respiratory safety. Ventilation could be through a window or an air purifying system."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/1275 ("10 Tips to Ensure a Safe and Clean Lasering Experience")

The D1 Safety Guide reinforces this in its fire-safety section:

> "Ensure that the workspace is well ventilated, so that the smoke can be discharged properly."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

For enclosed CO2 machines like P2, xTool documents two supported exhaust paths:

> "How to exhaust the smoke out of the machine? Way 1: Connect one end of the smoke exhaust pipe to the machine and the other end to the open air. The fan inside the machine exhausts the smoke out of the machine. Way 2: Connect the other end of the smoke exhaust pipe to a purifier. You can purchase xTool Smoke Purifier..."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/825

The F2 FAQ requires ventilation explicitly as a Class 4 laser safety precaution:

> "Use an air purifier or exhaust system for proper ventilation."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/2818

**Practical takeaway**: vent to open air (window duct) or use a dedicated fume/smoke purifier (e.g., xTool Smoke Purifier) every time you run the machine — do not rely on running it in a closed room "just this once," even for materials considered safe.

---

## 3. Fire Safety

Laser processing is inherently a fire risk because the process works by heating material to the point of vaporization, and most craft materials are flammable.

### Never leave a running laser unattended

This is repeated as the single most emphasized fire-safety rule across xTool's official documentation:

> "Keep watching: Never leave your laser unattended while it is running in case of fire."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/1275

> "Do not leave the machine unattended when it is working. If the machine works with incorrect settings and is left unattended for a long period of time, or if a mechanical or electrical fault occurs, a fire may be caused."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

### Fire watch / fire extinguisher

> "Always have a fire extinguisher nearby: Always keep a well-maintained CO2 fire extinguisher or a fire blanket within arm's reach of your laser. If it's a small flame, a spray bottle is enough."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/1275

The D1 Safety Guide adds housekeeping guidance to reduce fire spread risk:

> "Do not stack materials (especially organic ones, such as paper) around the machine. They may cause the spread of flames and increase the risk of material ignition."
>
> "Ensure that the area around the machine is clean without any cluttered flammable materials, explosives, or volatile solvents, such as acetone, alcohol, or gasoline. Keep a fire extinguisher and perform regular maintenance and inspection of it."
>
> "Clean the machine regularly. Excessive accumulation of residues and chippings from cutting and engraving may increase the risk of fire."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

### Vector cutting is the highest fire-risk operation

> "When the machine cuts or engraves a material, a high-density laser beam falls on the material, heating up the surface of the material to vaporize it without burning. But most materials are inherently flammable and may be ignited to form an open flame that may burn down the machine (even if it is made of flame-retardant materials) and its surroundings. From our experience, using laser to perform vector cutting is most likely to produce an open flame."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

### Material-specific fire-risk thresholds

- **P2 (CO2 55W)**: "When cutting plywood, you are advised not to cut a piece thicker than 12mm. The cutting of thick plywood may cause a fire." — official P2 FAQ, https://support.xtool.com/article/825
- **S1**: triggers a dedicated fire-risk error code (M53 F5) when a job combines a small design area (≤10x10cm), high power (≥90%), and low speed (≤10mm/s) — this specific combination concentrates enough heat in a small area to raise ignition risk. See support.xtool.com/article/1481.
- **Oily/resinous woods**: "Be careful about cutting oily or very resinous woods to prevent fire. Watch out while cutting wood at a speed under 2% at full power." — https://support.xtool.com/article/354
- **Cardboard/carton**: "Cuts well but may catch fire. Watch out for fire and be attentive to cutting." — https://support.xtool.com/article/354

### Emergency response

The F2 FAQ gives the most explicit emergency instruction found in official documentation:

> "Press the emergency stop button immediately if you see flames, smoke, or hear abnormal sounds."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/2818

---

## 4. Eye Protection

Laser light — including reflected/scattered light, not just the direct beam — can cause permanent eye damage, and the relevant wavelength ranges are NOT limited to visible light.

> "Eye protection is a necessity: Wear eye protection within the ranges of 190nm-460nm & 800nm-1100nm any time you are working with an active laser."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/1275

This dual range matters because xTool's product line spans multiple laser wavelengths: blue diode lasers (~445-460nm, covered by the first range) and infrared/fiber lasers (~1064nm, covered by the second range) are both in active use across D1 Pro, S1, M1, F1, and F1 Ultra.

For the D1 specifically:

> "Although a hood is provided to shield you from some laser light, you still need to wear the goggles to protect your eyes."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

For CO2 machines (P2), goggles are optional because the lid fully filters the beam, but if purchased, must match the CO2 wavelength — not the diode/fiber ranges above:

> "If I need to purchase protective goggles, what specifications are required? The lid is designed to filter laser light, so you don't need to buy separate protective goggles. However, if you do, make sure they have an OD4 protection level and are wavelength-rated for 10640nm."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/825

For F2 (dual blue-light 445nm + infrared 1064nm, Class 4):

> "Always wear laser goggles rated for 445 nm and 1064 nm laser... When operating with the protective enclosure open, stay ≥ 0.5 m away and wear goggles and a mask."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/2818

**Key principle**: laser safety eyewear must be rated for the SPECIFIC wavelength(s) your machine's laser module(s) emit — a pair of goggles rated for one wavelength range does not protect against another. Always check your exact machine's laser type(s) before buying eyewear, and re-check when using a dual-laser machine like F1/F1 Ultra/F2 across both its blue-diode and infrared/fiber modules.

---

## 5. Air Assist

Air assist is often thought of purely as a cut-quality feature, but it is also a safety feature: it actively displaces the hot combustion byproducts and flammable off-gas at the kerf, reducing sustained-flame risk and keeping optics clear of debris that could otherwise absorb stray energy and create a secondary heat source.

> "Air assist for cleaning cutting: Laser engraving and cutting can generate a significant amount of heat, especially when working with dense or thick materials. An air assist will ensure a cleaner cut and engrave in your workpiece."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/1275

The M2 cheat sheet frames it explicitly as a pre-job checklist item, alongside protective gear and material shielding:

> "Turn on air-assist. Air-assist blows away dust, smoke, and char, keeps the lens cleaner, and keeps the cutting path clear."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/3538

**Practical takeaway**: always enable air assist when your machine has it, especially for cutting operations (the highest fire-risk operation type) and on thick/dense materials where heat buildup is greatest.

---

## 6. General Safety Guidance

### Enclosure

> "Laser Enclosure for all-around protection: Utilizing an enclosure that surrounds the entire laser will eliminate the risk of accidental injury to people and pets by ensuring debris remains within the enclosure."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/1275

Machines vary in enclosure design: S1, P2, F1 Ultra, and F2 are enclosed (Class 1 or Class 4 with enclosure) by default, while D1 Pro is an open-frame machine requiring the user to provide their own shielding/enclosure and extra caution. Per the F2 FAQ's Class 4 classification logic:

> "Even though xTool F2 has a protective enclosure, it remains a Class 4 laser device because you can operate it with its protective enclosure open. According to laser safety standards, if the beam can be exposed under any condition, the device must be classified as Class 4. Always keep the protective enclosure closed and wear protective goggles during operation."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/2818

### Machine inspection and workspace condition

> "Check the machine for damage every time before you use it. Do not operate it in any way when any damage or defect is found. Ensure that the workspace is clean and flat."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

### Do not disassemble or modify

> "Do not disassemble the machine or change its structure in any way without authorization. Do not modify or decompile its operating system... Do not operate the machine when any part of it is removed. Removing any part may expose the laser system and cause damage to the machine."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

### Electrical safety

> "Do not disassemble the machine when it is connected to a power supply. Otherwise, an electric shock may be caused."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

### Keep children and pets away; secure the machine after use

> "Keep the device away from children and remove the key after use."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/2818 (F2 FAQ)

### Cleaning and maintenance reduce risk, not just improve output

> "Cleaning after lasering: It is important to clean the scraps, debris, and dust from your working area after each project. This will reduce the risk of fire and/or exposure to harmful fumes or particles."
>
> "Maintenance for a longer life span: Dust and debris can accumulate within the machine and cause reduced efficiency, decreased performance, and potential damage. It is important to follow a regular maintenance plan to ensure the longevity of the laser."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/1275

### Read the manual for your specific machine

> "Read the manual: Read the safety and operating instructions thoroughly prior to using the laser. Make sure you follow all the limits and know all the risks of operating your laser."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/1275

### Temperature operating range

> "The machine works properly at the temperature of 0°C to 35°C and can be stored properly at the temperature of 0°C to 45°C. Do not operate it at a temperature lower than 0°C."

Source: https://support.xtool.com/article/354

---

## Quick-Reference Checklist

Before starting any job:
- [ ] Confirm the material is not PVC/vinyl/chlorine-containing, and not on the prohibited list (chromium leather, PVB, PTFE, beryllium oxide, halogens, epoxy/phenolic resins, carbon fiber/fiberglass) or otherwise unknown-composition
- [ ] Ventilation/fume extraction running (open-air duct or smoke purifier)
- [ ] Air assist enabled
- [ ] Fire extinguisher (CO2) or fire blanket within arm's reach
- [ ] Correct-wavelength laser safety eyewear on hand if the enclosure doesn't fully shield you
- [ ] Enclosure/cover closed and workspace clear of clutter and flammable materials nearby
- [ ] Someone present and watching for the entire duration of the job — never walk away
- [ ] Machine inspected for visible damage before powering on

---

*Compiled from support.xtool.com/learning-center. All quotes verbatim from the cited official articles as of research date. Always consult your specific machine's printed manual and the latest version of these official articles, since safety guidance may be updated over time.*
